---
title: "Helen Webley-Brown"
image: "website-pic.jpg"
output:
  postcards::trestles
---
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```
I am a final-year S.M. student in Political Science at MIT with specializations in American Politics and Quantitative Methods. I am also an affiliate of the [*Center for American Political Studies*](https://caps.gov.harvard.edu/){style="color:#005354"} at Harvard University. In 2022, I graduated from Washington University in St. Louis with a BA in Political Science. This cycle, I will be re-applying to PhD programs.

Although my research focuses on the US, I grew up in London, England. I moved across the pond for college via the US-UK Fulbright Commission's wonderful [*Sutton Trust US Programme*](https://us.suttontrust.com/){style="color:#005354"}. My personal background has greatly shaped my commitment to inclusive pedagogy, social mobility, and widening access to higher education.

Currently, my research agenda fits into two broad buckets:

1.  The socio-political effects of interacting with authoritarian institutions, focusing on the [criminal legal system]{style="color:teal"} and [public housing authorities]{style="color:teal"}.
2.  How [emerging technologies]{style="color:teal"} change the way people experience and perceive the state.

At the intersection of this, I am interested in the participatory implications of tools like pretrial electronic monitors and ShotSpotter in policing, and Landlord Tech in subsidized housing. Parallel to studies of demobilization, I also ask what fosters successful collective action in these contentious spaces? That is, under what conditions can marginalized communities carve out democratic practices within the authoritarian systems that control, surveil, and punish them? I have two ongoing, early-stage projects that grapple with this question: one uses topic modelling to analyze Community Control Over Police Surveillance (CCOPS) legislation, while the other leverages a novel dataset to examine resident council emergence in public housing developments.
